title-group.article-title: Radiation-induced brain injury in non-human primates: A dual tracer PET study with [11C]MPC-6827 and [11C]PiB

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Radiology, Wake Forest University School of Medicine, Winston-Salem, NC, USA
bDepartment of Pathology, Section on Comparative Medicine, Wake Forest University School of Medicine, Winston-Salem, NC, USA

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: Radiation-induced brain injury (RIBI) and Alzheimer's disease (AD) share key pathological features, including β-amyloid (Aβ) plaque formation and microtubule (MT) destabilization, both contributing to neurodegeneration. This pilot study assessed Aβ deposition and MT stability in non-human primates (NHPs) exposed to ionizing radiation, utilizing [11C]PiB and [11C]MPC-6827 PET imaging to explore neurodegenerative mechanisms. Fourteen rhesus macaques, seven irradiated and seven controls underwent PET imaging. Tracers were synthesized and brain regions (ex. cingulate, hippocampus, and occipital lobe) were analyzed for tracer uptake. Although no statistically significant whole-brain differences in tracer uptake were found between irradiated and control groups, significant regional differences were observed in the occipital lobe, where irradiated NHPs exhibited higher [11C]MPC-6827 uptake (p < 0.0001), suggesting MT destabilization. No significant differences were found in [11C]PiB uptake. Correlation analysis revealed a slight positive association (Pearson r = 0.2866) between irradiation dose and [11C]MPC-6827 uptake. These findings suggest that irradiation-induced MT destabilization may be region-specific, offering insights into shared neurodegenerative pathways in RIBI and AD, highlighting the potential of [11C]MPC-6827 as a marker for early neuronal dysfunction in irradiated brains.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Radiation-induced brain injury (RIBI) represents a significant challenge in neurobiology, as exposure to ionizing radiation can initiate complex and persistent neurological damage (Connell and Hellman, 2009; Turnquist et al., 2020). Ionizing radiation triggers a multifaceted pathophysiological response characterized by oxidative stress, neuroinflammation, endothelial dysfunction, vascular damage, and blood-brain barrier disruption (Azzam et al., 2012; Baselet et al., 2019; Yahyapour et al., 2018). These mechanisms closely resemble the molecular processes observed in neurodegenerative diseases, particularly Alzheimer's disease (AD) (Begum et al., 2012). RIBI and AD share critical pathological features, including β-amyloid (Aβ) plaque modification and microtubule (MT) destabilization, contributing to synaptic dysfunction and neuronal loss. Given the overlapping pathways involved in RIBI and AD, including oxidative injury, protein aggregation, and cytoskeletal disintegration, investigating the impact of irradiation on Aβ deposition and MT stability is crucial in elucidating common mechanisms of neurodegeneration across these conditions.

In AD, Aβ plaques and neurofibrillary tangles disrupt cellular integrity by destabilizing MTs and impairing neuronal communication, ultimately leading to synaptic dysfunction and neuronal death (Hampel et al., 2021). Imaging of Aβ plaques has been extensively studied using [11C]Pittsburgh Compound-B ([11C]PiB), a radiotracer that binds selectively to fibrillar Aβ (Hong et al., 2014). However, while [11C]PiB provides valuable insight into Aβ dynamics, it does not capture other critical aspects of neurodegeneration, particularly those related to MT destabilization, an early hallmark of neuronal dysfunction.

MT stability plays a key role in maintaining neuronal architecture, and its disruption has been implicated in several neurodegenerative diseases, including AD (Damuka et al., 2021, 2022b; Kasibhatla et al., 2007). A new radiotracer, [11C]MPC-6827, has shown promise in assessing MT integrity by binding to β-tubulin with greater affinity in destabilized MTs (Bhoopal et al., 2023; Damuka et al., 2020, 2022a, 2022b; Sai et al., 2020a). This tracer offers a unique opportunity to investigate MT dynamics in the context of both RIBI and AD. Unlike [11C]PiB, which specifically targets Aβ plaques, [11C]MPC-6827 provides a broader perspective of neurodegeneration by detecting early changes in MT stability (Huang et al., 2022; Sai et al., 2020b). As such, the combination of [11C]PiB and [11C]MPC-6827 imaging can offer a more comprehensive assessment of neurodegenerative processes, particularly in conditions where both Aβ plaques and MT destabilization are involved.

This pilot study aims to investigate the effects of ionizing radiation on both Aβ deposition and MT stability in non-human primates (NHPs) using [11C]PiB and [11C]MPC-6827 PET imaging. By comparing irradiated and non-irradiated NHPs, we seek to understand how irradiation influences neurodegeneration, focusing on potential accelerations in Aβ plaque formation and MT destabilization. Specifically, we hypothesize that irradiation may enhance Aβ aggregation while simultaneously promoting MT destabilization, contributing to neurodegenerative processes that parallel AD pathology.

We utilized the NIH-supported Wake Forest Non-Human Primate Radiation Late Effects Cohort, a unique resource consisting of rhesus macaques (Macaca mulatta) exposed to a single dose of total body irradiation (Caudell et al., 2019; Michalson et al., 2019). These animals have been studied for over 17 years, providing a rare opportunity to examine long-term cognitive and neurodegenerative outcomes following irradiation exposure (Hanbury et al., 2016). Previous studies in this cohort have revealed cognitive impairment (Robbins et al., 2011), microvascular pathology (Andrews et al., 2018, 2020), and loss of white matter integrity (Johnson et al., 2024), suggesting that irradiation can have profound effects on brain function and structure.

Using [11C]PiB and [11C]MPC-6827 PET imaging, we sought to quantify regional Aβ deposition and MT destabilization, with a particular focus on the cingulate gyrus, posterior cingulate gyrus, hippocampus, parietal lobe, temporal lobe, and occipital lobe - regions that may be particularly vulnerable to irradiation-induced damage. By exploring the relationship between irradiation dose, Aβ plaques, and MT stability, this study seeks to uncover potential shared mechanisms between RIBI and AD. Ultimately, the findings from this research will expand our understanding of how irradiation exposure influences neurodegenerative processes, with implications for both RIBI and AD pathophysiology.

sec.title: Automated radiochemical synthesis of [11C]PiB and [11C]MPC-6827
sec.p: [11C]PiB and [11C]MPC-6827 were synthesized following previously published protocols (Bansode et al., 2023; Bhoopal et al., 2023; Damuka et al., 2024; Myburgh and Sai, 2024). Briefly, for [11C]PiB, a synthesis cassette was prepared with reagents primarily sourced from Trasis AllinOne. The precursor 6-OH-BTA-0 was dissolved in acetone, homogenized, and loaded onto a C18 Sep-Pak. The Sep-Pak was conditioned with ethanol and sterile water, and residual acetone was removed under argon at 55 °C. [11C]CO2 was generated in GE-PETtrace cyclotron, captured with molecular sieves (yielding ∼92.5 GBq), desorbed, and reacted with LiAlH4 in tetrahydrofuran (THF), reacted with hydriodic acid (HI) at 100 °C to produce [11C]MeI. The [11C]MeI was distilled, purified, and converted to [11C]MeOTf using a silver triflate column. The [11C]MeOTf gas was directed through the C18 Sep-Pak containing the precursor at 65 °C for methylation. The product was eluted and purified by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and the final [11C]PiB product was diluted with sterile water on a TRASIS AIO module (Myburgh and Sai, 2024).

The radiochemistry of [11C]MPC-6827 involved reacting the precursor desmethyl MPC-6827 with [11C]MeI in a NaOH/DMF solvent system within a GE FXM automated radiochemistry module. The reaction mixture was heated at 80 °C for 5 min. The product was purified via semipreparative HPLC with an acetonitrile-ammonium formate buffer system, followed by further purification using a C18 SepPak and 10% ethanol-saline elution. The chemical and radiochemical purity of [11C]MPC-6827 was confirmed by quality control HPLC prior to use in animal PET imaging and autoradiography studies (Kumar et al., 2018, 2020).

sec.title: Subjects
sec.p: Fourteen young adult male rhesus monkeys were selected from the Wake Forest Radiation Late Effects Cohort, seven of which had received prior total body irradiation, and seven unirradiated age-matched controls. These 14 animals were between 7.38 and 9.61 years old (mean 8.32 years old). The irradiated animals had received 6.4–8.0 Gy (mean 7.2 Gy) of total body irradiation 5 years prior. A summary table detailing the gender, body weight, age, and radiation sources of the study animals is provided in Supplementary Table 1. All procedures were conducted with approval by the WFUSOM Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee. Wake Forest University is committed to providing a high-quality program of animal care in compliance with state and federal Animal Welfare Acts, as well as the standards and policies of the Public Health Service. WFUSOM has an Assurance on file in the Office of Laboratory Animal Welfare, Office of the Director, National Institutes of Health, which accepts responsibility for the humane care and use of animals (OLAW #A-3391-01). The Laboratory Animal Care Program of WFUSOM complies with the “Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals,” all provisions of the Animal Welfare Act, and has been accredited by the Association for Assessment and Accreditation of Laboratory Animal Care, International (AAALAC) since April 8, 1966 (AAALAC File #8).

sec.title: NHP PET imaging
sec.p: All fourteen rhesus monkeys underwent PET/CT imaging using [11C]PiB and [11C]MPC-6827. Prior to PET imaging, each animal underwent a structural magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan under general anesthesia (1–2% isoflurane) using a 3.0-T Siemens Skyra Clinical Magnetic Resonance Scanner (Siemens AG). On the day of the PET scan, monkeys were anesthetized with ketamine (10 mg/kg) and transported to the Wake Forest TIP Center, where they were maintained under 1–2% isoflurane anesthesia. Monkeys were positioned in a GE PET/CT discovery scanner, and a catheter was placed in the external saphenous vein for tracer injection and fluid maintenance. Body temperature was maintained at 40 °C with a warm-air circulating blanket, and vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, temperature, SpO2, and EtCO2) were monitored throughout the scanning procedure.

Anesthetized control and irradiated monkeys received intravenous doses of [11C]PiB (∼0.296 ± 0.01 GBq) or [11C]MPC-6827 (∼0.333 ± 0.01 GBq). Dynamic brain PET scans were performed for 20 min ([11C]PiB) or 60 min ([11C]MPC-6827). Post-scanning, raw DICOM data were binned, and Standard Uptake Values (SUV) for [11C]MPC-6827 and SUVr for [11C]PiB were measured (cerebellum as reference region). All image processing was conducted blinded to the monkey information. Whole-brain MRI images were acquired and processed to define anatomical spherical regions of interest (ROIs). Brain atlas processing was performed using the NIC Primate Brain Atlas for accurate anatomical reference and ROI alignment (Styner et al., 2007). These ROIs included the right and left anterior cingulate gyrus, posterior cingulate gyrus, hippocampus, parietal lobe, temporal lobe, frontal lobe, and occipital lobe (Supplementary Fig. 1). The ROIs were delineated using PMOD software (Version 3.5, PMOD Technologies LLC, Zurich, Switzerland).

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: All statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism (version 10; GraphPad Software, La Jolla, CA, USA). For the analysis of in vivo PET images of whole-brain uptake, an unpaired t-test was used to compare differences between the groups. A two-way ANOVA was conducted for regional radiotracer uptake data, followed by Šídák's multiple comparisons test to assess specific differences between brain regions. Correlations between irradiation dose and tracer uptake were evaluated using Pearson's correlation coefficients (r) for each tracer separately.

sec.title: Whole brain analysis
sec.p:
The uptake of the radiotracers [11C]PiB and [11C]MPC-6827 was analyzed in irradiation-exposed and control group monkeys. Representative PET/MR co-registered images from one monkey are shown in Fig. 1A. Compared to control scans, the irradiation-exposed groups demonstrated a trend towards higher [11C]MPC-6827 uptake across the examined brain regions. However, with a limited sample size (n = 7), whole-brain PET/MRI analysis did not reveal statistically significant differences between irradiation-exposed and control groups for either radiotracer. Specifically, the p-value for [11C]MPC-6827 uptake was 0.6909, while that for [11C]PiB was 0.3269, indicating only marginal trends toward increased microtubule destabilization and decreased β-amyloid deposition in the irradiation-exposed group, respectively (Fig. 1B and C).Fig. 1
(A) Representative PET/CT brain images of control and irradiated NHPs following intravenous injection of [11C]MPC-6827 (∼0.333 ± 0.01 GBq) and [ 11C]PiB (∼0.296 ± 0.01 GBq) for 0−120 min. (B) Corresponding SUVr uptake of [11C]PiB and (C) SUV of [ 11C]MPC-6827.Fig. 1

sec.title: Regional brain analysis
sec.p:
When specific brain regions were examined, significant differences were observed only in the occipital lobe, where the [11C]MPC-6827 tracer showed markedly higher uptake in the irradiation-exposed NHPs compared to controls (p < 0.0001). This finding suggests that the occipital lobe may be particularly vulnerable to irradiation-induced microtubule destabilization. However, no significant differences were found in other regions such as the anterior cingulate gyrus, posterior cingulate gyrus, hippocampus, parietal lobe, or temporal lobe for either tracer. The [11C]PiB SUVR values were slightly lower in the irradiation-exposed group, with p-values ranging from 0.8180 to 0.9870 across these regions (Fig. 2A). The [11C]MPC-6827 SUV values varied, with the occipital lobe showing the most significant increase (p < 0.0001), while other regions exhibited p-values ranging from 0.3481 to 0.9961 (Fig. 2B). These regional differences highlight the potential for [11C]MPC-6827 to detect microtubule destabilization, particularly in regions more susceptible to irradiation damage, such as the occipital lobe.Fig. 2
Representative Standardized uptake values of (A) [11C]PiB and (B) [11C]MPC-6827 (body weight {g/ml}) from various brain regions, obtained from dynamic PET scans on control and irradiated NHP, groups (n = 7/group).Fig. 2

sec.title: Correlation with irradiation dose
sec.p:
To further understand the impact of irradiation on tracer uptake, we performed a correlation analysis between irradiation dose and tracer uptake. The results revealed a slightly negative correlation (Pearson r = −0.4854) for [11C]PiB uptake (Fig. 3A) and a slightly positive correlation (Pearson r = 0.2866) for [11C]MPC-6827 uptake (Fig. 3B) in the irradiation-exposed group. These findings suggest that higher irradiation doses may be associated with increased microtubule destabilization, as indicated by the [11C]MPC-6827 tracer, while potentially reducing Aβ plaque formation, as indicated by the [11C]PiB tracer.Fig. 3
Correlation of irradiation dose (Gy) with (A) SUVr of [11C]PiB and (B) SUV (body weight {g/ml}) of [11C]MPC-6827 in the whole brain regions.Fig. 3

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study aimed to investigate the effects of irradiation exposure on microtubule destabilization and β-amyloid deposition in young adult rhesus monkeys using the PET tracers [11C]MPC-6827 and [11C]PiB. The results revealed trends suggesting increased microtubule destabilization, as evidenced by higher [11C]MPC-6827 uptake, particularly in the occipital lobe, following irradiation exposure. In contrast, no statistically significant changes in [11C]PiB uptake were observed, indicating that β-amyloid deposition may not be as responsive to irradiation-induced changes in this young adult cohort of monkeys. These results suggest that microtubule destabilization is an early and sensitive marker of irradiation-induced brain injury, while β-amyloid accumulation may occur later or not be as responsive in this age group.

The significant regional differences in the occipital lobe are intriguing. One possible explanation is the heightened vulnerability of this region to irradiation-induced damage due to due to variations in vascular density, metabolic activity, and neuronal architecture. Prior studies have also demonstrated the occipital lobe's sensitivity to oxidative stress and vascular dysfunction likely contributes to microtubule destabilization (Carvalho and Moreira, 2018). These findings align with observations from AD models, where the occipital lobe has shown distinct pathological changes during disease progression. For instance, Love et al. (2014), reported that increased reactive oxygen species (ROS) overproduction contributes to amyloid-beta (Aβ) deposition in vessel walls, a hallmark of cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA), particularly in the occipital lobe. This feature is commonly observed in both non-demented elderly individuals and AD patients. These studies suggest that the occipital lobe may be more susceptible to both irradiation-induced damage and neurodegenerative changes associated with AD.

One of the most interesting aspects of this study is the use of young adult rhesus monkeys, aged 7–9 years, equivalent to approximately 20–30 years in humans. Significant β-amyloid changes are uncommon in this age range in humans, as amyloid deposition is typically associated with aging and Alzheimer's disease. The detection of microtubule destabilization and marginal β-amyloid changes in these young monkeys may reflect an accelerated aging phenotype induced by irradiation exposure, a hypothesis supported by previous research (Schaaf et al., 2023). This may help explain the early neurodegenerative processes observed, particularly the increased vulnerability of the occipital lobe to irradiation-induced damage.

A key finding of this study is the direct connection observed between irradiation exposure and microtubule destabilization in a non-human primate model. The ability to detect such changes using [11C]MPC-6827 PET imaging highlights the potential of microtubule destabilization as an early marker of irradiation-induced brain damage. Microtubules play a vital role in maintaining neuronal structure and function, and their destabilization has been implicated in many neurological conditions. However, this study shows, for the first time in an irradiation exposure model, that specific brain regions may be more vulnerable to microtubule destabilization, suggesting a regional susceptibility to irradiation-induced cytoskeletal damage.

The analysis of dose-dependent effects revealed a trend towards increased microtubule destabilization in monkeys exposed to higher irradiation doses (7–8 Gy) compared to those exposed to less (6–7 Gy). Although not statistically significant, (p = 0.3055), these findings suggest a potential dose-response relationship, where increased irradiation exposure exacerbates cytoskeletal damage. Future studies with larger sample sizes may be able to confirm a dose threshold at which microtubule destabilization becomes more pronounced.

In terms of β-amyloid deposition, the lack of significant differences in [11C]PiB uptake across brain regions and irradiation doses suggests that [11C]PiB may not be sensitive enough to detect subtle or early-stage amyloid changes in this context. This is possibly due to the younger age of the cohort, where amyloid deposition is typically less prominent. Moreover, irradiation-induced amyloid changes may require more time to manifest, or the morphological changes caused by irradiation may be difficult to detect using [11C]PiB.

The ability of [11C]MPC-6827 to detect early microtubule changes presents a new approach to understanding radiation-induced brain injury. As microtubules are critical to neuronal integrity, their destabilization could lead to long-term cognitive and neurological deficits. These findings have broader implications, suggesting that microtubule-targeted imaging tools like [11C]MPC-6827 could play a critical role in assessing the immediate effects of irradiation on the brain, with potential for development in clinical use for irradiation-exposed patients.

This study highlights a shift in focus from traditional neurodegenerative markers, such as amyloid plaques, to cytoskeletal changes, which could offer earlier insights into both acute irradiation damage and long-term neurodegenerative processes, including AD. Early detection of microtubule destabilization could facilitate more timely therapeutic interventions aimed at stabilizing microtubules, potentially mitigating the long-term effects of irradiation and offering a new avenue for therapeutic strategies in RIBI patients. In the context of AD, these microtubule-targeted approaches could also help monitor disease progression and evaluate the efficacy of microtubule-stabilizing therapies.

In summary, this study underscores the importance of cytoskeletal integrity in the context of radiation-induced brain injury. The results suggest that microtubule destabilization may be a more immediate and sensitive marker of irradiation-induced damage compared to β-amyloid deposition, especially in younger subjects. The regional specificity of [11C]MPC-6827 uptake, particularly in the occipital lobe, shows the utility of this tracer in studying the neurobiological effects of irradiation exposure. These findings provide a foundation for future research exploring the relationship between irradiation and cytoskeletal damage, including potential future therapies targeting microtubule destabilization. Incorporating other imaging tracers, such as those targeting Synaptic Vesicle Glycoprotein 2 A (SV2A) or neuroinflammation ([18F]FEPPA), could provide complementary insights. Longitudinal studies using larger cohorts are essential to establish dose thresholds and temporal patterns of irradiation-induced changes.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: These preliminary feasibility studies are the first to demonstrate the real-time changes in both Aβ accumulation and microtubule stability in a non-human primate model of radiation exposure. Our findings suggest that microtubule PET imaging with [11C]MPC-6827 may serve as a valuable biomarker for assessing microtubule alterations in irradiation-exposed brains, providing important insights into the relationship between irradiation exposure and cytoskeletal dynamics. The ability to assess cytoskeletal damage at an early stage presents significant research and clinical opportunities. Future studies should aim to expand on these findings by increasing sample size and exploring varied levels of irradiation exposure, which may help further clarify the role of microtubules in radiation-induced brain injury and inform the development of relevant therapeutic strategies. Next steps include longitudinal imaging to monitor disease progression, integrating behavioral assessments, testing microtubule-stabilizing therapies, and incorporating additional tracers (e.g., for neuroinflammation and synaptic density) to gain deeper understand RIBI mechanisms.

Overall, this study enhances our understanding of how irradiation exposure affects brain structure and function by emphasizing the critical role of microtubule stability. By focusing on cytoskeletal changes, the research provides a new perspective on irradiation-induced brain injury and sets the stage for future innovations in imaging and therapy relating to preservation of the brain's structural integrity.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Naresh Damuka: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation. George W. Schaaf: Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mack Miller: Validation, Software, Data curation. Caleb Bradley: Writing – review & editing, Software, Data curation. Bhuvanachandra Bhoopal: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Data curation. Ivan Krizan: Conceptualization. Krishna K. Gollapelli: Data curation. Christopher T. Whitlow: Data curation, Conceptualization. J. Mark Cline: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Kiran K. Solingapuram Sai: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors have no conflict of interests to disclose.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: Data will be made available on request.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: This work was supported by NIH/NIAID funding (U01 AI150578, U19 AI67798). The authors would like to thank and appreciate the Cline lab technical staff, the Wake Forest University Animal Resource Program's clinical support and husbandry staff, and Translational Imaging Program (UM1TR004929).

fn.label: Appendix A
fn.p: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ynirp.2025.100245.
